title: Wire Gauge Calculator by Amperage & Distance | Hacé Cuentas
h1: What Wire Gauge Do I Need Based on Amperage and Distance?
description: Calculate electrical wire gauge (mm²) by amperage, distance, and voltage. Prevent voltage drop over 3%. Includes breaker sizing recommendations.
intro: Choosing the **correct wire gauge** (mm²) is **critical for electrical safety**: a wire that's **too small** for the current it carries will **overheat, melt the insulation, and cause electrical fires**. Wire sizing depends on **two factors**: (1) the **current-carrying capacity** of the wire (how many amps it can safely handle without overheating) and (2) the **allowable voltage drop** (maximum 3% for residential, 5% for industrial installations). This calculator evaluates both requirements and gives you the **smallest commercial wire gauge** (1.5 | 2.5 | 4 | 6 | 10 | 16 | 25 | 35 mm²) that meets **safety standards with a safety margin**. It also recommends the appropriate **circuit breaker size** and calculates the **actual voltage drop** in your installation. Works for 220V single-phase (standard residential) and 380V three-phase (industrial/large equipment). Based on **AEA 90364** (Argentine Electrical Association standards) — the electrical code used in Argentina.
keyTakeaway: For **220V residential**: **1.5 mm² wire = 14 A (lighting)**, **2.5 mm² = 21 A (standard outlets)**, **4 mm² = 28 A (air conditioning)**, **6 mm² = 36 A (electric ovens)**. For **distances over 20 m**: go up one wire size. Maximum voltage drop: **3%**.
useCases: Installing an **air conditioning unit** in a room far from the electrical panel — determining the correct wire size.
Doing the **electrical wiring** for a house addition or new construction.
**Adding a new circuit** for an electric oven, water heater, or motor.
Your **circuit breaker keeps tripping** and you want to know if the wire is undersized.
Licensed electrician preparing **project plans and technical specifications**.

fields.label: Equipment/circuit amperage (A)
fields.help: If you have the equipment, divide watts by 220. Example: 4,400 W ÷ 220 = 20 A.

fields.label: Cable distance (meters, one direction only)
fields.help: Distance from the breaker panel to the equipment, one-way only (calculator doubles for single-phase).

fields.label: Maximum allowable voltage drop (%)
fields.help: 3% for residential (AEA standard), 5% for industrial, 1% for sensitive equipment.

dataUpdate.notes: Wire gauge by amperage and distance. AEA 90364 standards stable.

example.title: Real-world example: wire for a 3,000 BTU air conditioner (1,200 W consumption) 25 m from the breaker panel
steps: **Amperage**: 1,200 W ÷ 220 V ≈ **5.5 A continuous**. With inrush current + safety margin: **size for 20 A** (breaker + wire).
**Distance**: 25 m from breaker panel to AC unit.
**Wire gauge formula**: S = (2 × L × A × ρ_copper) / ΔV = (2 × 25 × 20 × 0.0178) / 6.6 = **2.70 mm²**.
**Commercial wire size**: round up to next standard → **4 mm²** (2.5 mm² lacks sufficient margin for 20 A and voltage drop).
**Wire capacity at 4 mm²**: 28 A (twice the actual consumption with safety margin).
**Actual voltage drop with 4 mm²**: (2 × 25 × 20 × 0.0178) / 4 = **4.45 V = 2.02%** ✓ (under 3% limit).
**Breaker size**: 20 A (just above the inrush current, well below wire capacity).
example.result: For a 3,000 BTU AC unit 25 m away: **4 mm² wire + 20 A breaker**. Voltage drop is 2.02%, within the 3% AEA standard. Approximate cost: $8,000–$12,000 for 25 m of cable.

explanation:
## Formulas

### Voltage drop (single-phase 220V)

```
ΔV = 2 × L × A × ρ / S

Where:
  ΔV = voltage drop (V)
  L  = cable length (m, one direction)
  A  = amperage (A)
  ρ  = copper resistivity = 0.0178 Ω·mm²/m at 20°C
  S  = conductor cross-section (mm²)
  2  = factor for round-trip current path
```

### For three-phase 380V

```
ΔV = √3 × L × A × ρ / S
```

### Solving for wire gauge

```
S = (2 × L × A × ρ) / ΔV_max
```

Where `ΔV_max = V × (maxDropPercent / 100)` → for 220V and 3%: `ΔV_max = 6.6 V`.

## Wire ampacity by gauge (AEA 90364)

| Gauge (mm²) | Capacity A (in conduit) | Capacity A (open air) | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1.0** | 10 | 14 | Small lighting circuits |
| **1.5** | 14 | 19 | **Standard lighting (IU)** |
| **2.5** | 21 | 26 | **Standard outlets (TU)** |
| **4.0** | 28 | 36 | **Air conditioning, general outlets (TUG)** |
| **6.0** | 36 | 45 | Electric ovens, water heaters |
| **10.0** | 50 | 63 | Kitchen circuits, special outlets (TUE) |
| **16.0** | 68 | 85 | Main service entrance (small house) |
| **25.0** | 89 | 112 | Main service entrance (large house) |
| **35.0** | 110 | 140 | Multi-unit building service |
| **50.0** | 134 | 170 | Generator connection |
| **70.0** | 171 | 215 | Industrial distribution panels |
| **95.0** | 207 | 260 | Industrial distribution |

## Residential circuit classifications per AEA

| Circuit type | Wire gauge | Breaker | Typical load |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IUA** (general lighting) | 1.5 mm² | 10 A | 1,500 W lighting |
| **IUG** (general lighting) | 1.5 mm² | 10 A | 1,500 W lighting |
| **TUA** (standard outlets) | 2.5 mm² | 20 A | 4,400 W (computers, TVs) |
| **TUG** (general outlets) | 2.5 mm² | 20 A | 4,400 W |
| **TUE** (kitchen/laundry special) | 4.0 mm² | 25 A | 5,500 W (washer, microwave) |
| **Air conditioning** | 4.0–6.0 mm² | 20–25 A | 4,500–5,500 W |
| **Electric water heater** | 4.0–6.0 mm² | 25 A | 5,500 W |
| **Electric oven** | 6.0 mm² | 32 A | 7,000 W |
| **Main service entrance** | 16 mm² | 40–63 A | Whole house |

## Voltage, amperage, and power

```
P (W)  = V × A       (single-phase)
P (W)  = √3 × V × A × cos(φ)   (three-phase)
A      = P / V

Residential examples:
  100 W light bulb at 220V  → 0.45 A
  LED TV 150 W              → 0.68 A
  Microwave 1,200 W         → 5.45 A
  Water heater 2,200 W      → 10.0 A
  Electric oven 3,500 W     → 15.9 A
  AC unit 3,000 BTU 1,200W  → 5.45 A
  Electric range 7,000 W    → 31.8 A (very high!)
```

## Why voltage drop matters

**Voltage drop** is the loss of voltage along the wire due to its **electrical resistance**. If you have 220V at the panel but only 210V reaches the equipment, that's a 10V drop = 4.5%.

### Problems from excessive voltage drop

- **Poor equipment performance**: lights dim, motors struggle to start.
- **Overheating**: motors draw more current trying to compensate → wire overheats.
- **Electronics fail**: computers, TVs, and control boards are damaged by low voltage.
- **Wasted energy**: equipment runs longer and hotter to do the same job.

### Maximum allowable drop (AEA standards)

| Application | Max voltage drop |
|---|---|
| **Residential** | **3%** |
| Commercial | 4% |
| Industrial | 5% |
| Motor starting (inrush) | 6% |
| Sensitive equipment (servers, UPS) | 1% |

## Circuit breaker selection

The **circuit breaker** (or thermal breaker) protects the wire from overheating. It must satisfy:

```
A_equipment  ≤  A_breaker  ≤  A_wire_capacity
```

**Example**: 15 A equipment, 2.5 mm² wire (21 A capacity) → **16 A breaker** (next size above equipment, below wire capacity).

**Standard breaker sizes** (A): **6, 10, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, 125, 160**.

## Differential breaker (RCD/GFCI)

Every residential installation **must have a differential breaker of 30 mA** (mandatory by AEA). It protects against **electrocution** by cutting power if current leaks to ground. **It does not replace the thermal breaker** — they work together.

## Wire color codes per AEA

| Color | Function |
|---|---|
| **Brown** / Black | Live (phase), 220V |
| **Light blue** | Neutral |
| **Green/yellow striped** | Ground (earth) |
| **Red / Gray** | Second phase (split-phase or three-phase) |

True ground is **always green-and-yellow stripe**. A wire that's only green or only yellow is not ground.

## Wire types

| Type | Application |
|---|---|
| **NYM** / NYA | Fixed in-wall installations |
| **Flexible workshop cable** | Temporary runs, appliance cords |
| **Underground cable** | Buried runs, extra insulation |
| **Fire-resistant** | High-rise buildings (smoke reduction) |
| **Bare copper/aluminum** | Ground connections, inside conduit |
| **Armored (MC)** | Industrial, high-interference areas |

## Indicative pricing (Argentina 2026)

| Wire size | Cost per meter (100m spool) |
|---|---|
| 1.5 mm² | $800 – $1,200 |
| 2.5 mm² | $1,200 – $1,800 |
| 4 mm² | $1,800 – $2,800 |
| 6 mm² | $2,500 – $3,800 |
| 10 mm² | $4,000 – $5,500 |
| 16 mm² | $6,000 – $8,500 |

Common brands: **Imsa, Pirelli, Delcable, Prysmian**.

## Installation tips

1. **Never overload an outlet**: avoid power strips for air conditioners or microwaves.
2. **Wires in conduit**: cables run inside plastic or rigid conduit.
3. **No splices inside conduit**: splices only in junction boxes.
4. **Strict color code**: professional electricians always follow AEA colors.
5. **Subpanel for large loads**: homes over 1,000 sq ft benefit from a second panel near heavy users (kitchen, laundry).
6. **Inspect every 5 years**: wire insulation degrades; check for loose connections and heat.

## Common residential setups

### Small house (70 m² / 750 sq ft)

- 1 lighting circuit: 1.5 mm² + 10 A breaker
- 1 outlet circuit: 2.5 mm² + 20 A breaker
- 1 kitchen/laundry circuit: 4 mm² + 25 A breaker
- 1 AC circuit (if equipped): 4 mm² + 20 A breaker
- Main service: 16 mm² + 40 A breaker + 25 A / 30 mA differential

### Medium house (150 m² / 1,600 sq ft)

- 2 lighting circuits (6+6 A)
- 2 outlet circuits (20+20 A)
- 1 kitchen special (25 A)
- 2 AC circuits (25+25 A)
- 1 water heater (32 A)
- Main service: 25 mm² + 63 A breaker + 63 A / 30 mA differential

## Common mistakes

1. **Oversizing for false safety**: jumping from 1.5 to 4 mm² is wasteful. Use the AEA table.
2. **Ignoring distance**: 30 m with 2.5 mm² at 20 A causes over 5% drop (non-compliant).
3. **Breaker larger than wire**: NEVER. If wire is 2.5 mm² (21 A capacity), max breaker is 20 A.
4. **Skipping the differential**: not optional — it's a legal and safety requirement.
5. **Poor ground connections**: most older houses have inadequate grounding. Test with a ground meter.

## Related calculators

- [Electrical energy consumption (kWh)](/calculadora-consumo-electrico-kwh-factura-luz) — estimate your utility bill.
- [Air conditioner BTU sizing](/calculadora-aire-acondicionado-frigorias-ambiente) — calculate load before wiring.
- [Heating watts by room](/calculadora-calefacion-watts-kcal-ambiente) — electric heaters draw significant amperage.
- [Construction cost per square meter](/calculadora-costo-m2-construccion-argentina) — full project budgeting.

faq.q: How do I know what wire gauge I need for my circuit?
faq.a: Use three factors: (1) **amperage** of the load (divide watts by 220V), (2) **distance** from breaker to equipment, and (3) **voltage drop limit** (3% for residential). For example, a 20 A load 25 m away needs 4 mm² wire to stay under 3% drop. This calculator handles the math automatically.

faq.q: How many amps can a 2.5 mm² wire safely handle?
faq.a: According to AEA 90364: **21 A inside conduit** (the standard for residential). **26 A in open air**. In practice, a 20 A breaker is used with 2.5 mm² wire, allowing equipment up to 20 A = 4,400 W at 220V. This is the most common residential outlet circuit.

faq.q: What is voltage drop and why does it matter?
faq.a: Voltage drop is the loss of voltage traveling through a wire due to resistance. If 220V leaves the panel but only 210V reaches the equipment, that's a 4.5% drop. Excessive drop causes lights to dim, motors to overheat, and electronics to fail. Residential code limits it to 3%.

faq.q: How far can I run 2.5 mm² wire at 15 amps before voltage drop becomes a problem?
faq.a: For 3% maximum drop at 220V: approximately **18 meters round-trip distance** (9 m one way). Beyond that, you'll exceed 3% drop. For longer runs, use 4 mm² wire instead, which allows roughly **28 m one-way** at the same amperage.

faq.q: Should I use copper or aluminum wire for my home electrical installation?
faq.a: **Copper** is the standard for residential work: it has lower resistance, better conductivity, and more durable connections. **Aluminum** is cheaper (~40% less) but has higher resistance, requiring thicker gauge (~50% larger) to match copper's capacity. Aluminum is used only for large outdoor distribution lines or sections over 35 mm². For homes: **always copper**.

faq.q: What's the difference between single-phase (220V) and three-phase (380V) electrical service?
faq.a: **Single-phase 220V**: 2 wires (phase + neutral) + ground. Standard residential. Power = V × A. Good for loads up to ~40 A. **Three-phase 380V**: 4 wires (3 phases + neutral) + ground. For industrial, large commercial, or high consumption. Power = √3 × V × A × power factor. More efficient for motors and large continuous loads.

faq.q: Why does my circuit breaker keep tripping?
faq.a: Three main reasons: (1) **Overload** — too many amps for the circuit. Remove devices. (2) **Short circuit** — a live wire touching neutral or ground. Call an electrician (fire risk). (3) **Old/faulty breaker** — degrades every 10–15 years. Never replace it with a larger breaker unless you also upgrade the wire. Always investigate the cause first.

faq.q: Is a GFCI (differential) breaker really necessary?
faq.a: **Yes, it's mandatory** per AEA 90364 in all residential installations since 1990. A 30 mA differential breaker detects leaks to ground and cuts power instantly — protecting against electrocution. **Without one**: (1) serious electrocution risk, (2) legal non-compliance, (3) insurance claims may be denied, (4) utility may refuse connection. Install it in your main panel.

faq.q: What happens if I use undersized wire for my circuit?
faq.a: Extremely dangerous. Undersized wire **overheats** from resistance (P = I²×R), which **melts the insulation**, causing **short circuits and electrical fires**. The breaker may not trip immediately if the overload is gradual. This is the #1 cause of residential electrical fires in Argentina. Always respect AEA sizing tables — it's literally a life-or-death decision.

faq.q: What are the electrical wire color codes, and why do they matter?
faq.a: **Brown/Black** = live (phase, 220V). **Light blue** = neutral. **Green-yellow striped** = ground. **Red/Gray** = second phase. Color codes **must be followed strictly** — wrong colors create confusion, dangerous mistakes, and shocks. A professional electrician always respects the AEA color standard.
